Transcriptional profile of Aspergillus fumigatus under azole treatment

2022-01-21


1 STUDY DESIGN

  • 4 lineages
    • A
    • B
    • CT (never exposed to drug)
    • D
  • Conditions
    • EVO (under voriconazole 1.0mg/L and then 2.0 mg/L for 5 passages apiece)
    • REV (grown for 5 generations on untreated media)
  • Time points
    • 0h (no drug)
    • 1h (1.0 mg/L Voriconazole)
    • 4h (1.0 mg/L Voriconazole)


2 METHODS

  • It was used GEO2RNAseq docker container to host the analysis

    • https://www.openagrar.de/receive/openagrar_mods_00051275

    • https://hub.docker.com/r/xentrics/geo2rnaseq


Docker

Creates a virtual environment that contains all tools described in GEO2RNAseq.

DOCKER_PORT=1060
DOCKER_NAME=geo2rnaseq_EvoRev_afu
IMAGE=xentrics/geo2rnaseq
docker run -d --rm \
    --user root:root \
    -e PASSWORD=password \
    -e "USERID=$(id -u)" \
    -e "GROUPID=2000" \
    -p $DOCKER_PORT:8787 \
    -v /sbidata/apereira/analysis/rnaseq_phillip_EvoRev/:/sbidata/apereira/analysis/rnaseq_phillip_EvoRev \
    -v /sbidata/abarber/azole_evo/:/sbidata/abarber/azole_evo \
    --name $DOCKER_NAME \
    $IMAGE \
    /init

Raw

Fastqc reports basics statistics about reads.

# Import files path
fastq_files <- list.files("/sbidata/abarber/azole_evo/02_rnaseq/01_raw_fastq",
                          full.names = TRUE,
                          pattern = "fq")
# Set variables
ncores = 64
workers = 64
paired <- T

# Run fastqc on raw data
fq_raw_res <- run_FastQC(
  files  = fastq_files,
  outDir = file.path("quality", "raw"),
  cpus = ncores,
  workers = workers
)

# Importing A. fumigatus reference genome, genome indexed and genes description
ref_genome <- "/sbidata/apereira/analysis/rnaseq_phillip_EvoRev/ref_files/FungiDB-55_AfumigatusAf293_Genome.fasta"
map_index  <- "/sbidata/apereira/analysis/rnaseq_phillip_EvoRev/ref_files/index/afu_genome.index"
annot <- "/sbidata/apereira/analysis/rnaseq_phillip_EvoRev/ref_files/afu_af293.annot"

Trimming

Trimmomatic removes remaining adapters and low quality reads

# Original run_trimommatic doesn't work properly in paired-end
source("/sbidata/apereira/analysis/rnaseq_phillip_EvoRev/src/code_fix.R")
trim_res <- run_Trimmomatic_fix(
  files     = fastq_files,
  outDir    = file.path("fastq"),
  is.paired = TRUE,
  compress  = TRUE,
  cpus = ncores,
  workers = workers
)

Trimmed

We run fastqc on trimmed reads to check how much was lost.

# Running FastQC on trimmed fastq files

fq_raw_res <- run_FastQC(
  files  = fastq_files,
  outDir = file.path("quality", "filter"),
  extend = TRUE,
  cpus = ncores,
  workers = workers
)

Mapping

Hisat2 aligns reads on reference genome in order to quantify transcripts.

map_hisat_res <- run_Hisat2(
  files     = fastq_files,
  index     = map_index,
  outDir    = file.path("mapping", "hisat"),
  is.paired = paired,
  cpus      = ncores,
  workers   = workers
)

Transcripts count

Feature Counts assigns reads to genes described in gtf file.

count_res <- run_featureCounts(
  files       = bamFiles,
  annotation  = annot.gtf,
  outDir      = file.path("counting"),
  featureType = "exon",
  IDtype      = "gene_id",
  isPairedEnd = paired,
  cpus = ncores,
  workers = workers
)

PCA

PCA is a dimension reduction approach that evaluates samples similarity

counts.no.rRNA.mrn <- get_mrn(annotate.counts.no.rRNA)

pca.strainxCond <- tbl4pca %>%
  separate(name, 
           c("strain", "condition", "timePoint", "replicate", "pairEnd"),
           sep = "_",
           remove = FALSE) %>%
  filter(pairEnd == "1.fq.gz") %>% 
  mutate(samples = paste(strain, 
                         condition, 
                         timePoint,
                         replicate,
                         sep = "_")) %>% 
  mutate(cond2 = paste(strain, condition, sep = "_")) %>%
  select(cond2) %>% pull()

pca.timePoint <- tbl4pca %>%
  separate(name, 
           c("strain", "condition", "timePoint", "replicate", "pairEnd"),
           sep = "_",
           remove = FALSE) %>%
  filter(pairEnd == "1.fq.gz") %>% 
  mutate(samples = paste(strain, 
                         condition, 
                         timePoint,
                         replicate,
                         sep = "_")) %>% 
  select(timePoint) %>% pull()

pca_res <- make_PCA_plot(
file        = file.path("PCA_strainxCond_timepoint2.pdf"), # output file names
counts      = annotate.counts.no.rRNA,
conds       = pca.strainxCond,
shapes      = pca.timePoint,
add_eclipse = TRUE
)

DEG: Time course

  • For a control and treatment time series, one can use a design formula containing the condition factor, the time factor, and the interaction of the two.

  • In this case, using the likelihood ratio test with a reduced model which does not contain the interaction terms will test whether the condition induces a change in gene expression at any time point after the reference level time point (time 0).

coldata <- tbl4design %>%
  separate(name, 
           c("strain0", "condition0", "timePoint", "replicate", "pairEnd"),
           sep = "_",
           remove = TRUE) %>%
  filter(pairEnd == "1.fq.gz") %>% 
  mutate(samples = paste(strain0, 
                         condition0, 
                         timePoint,
                         replicate,
                         sep = "_")) %>%
  mutate(strain = paste(strain0, condition0, sep = "_")) %>% 
  select(samples, strain, timePoint) %>%
  mutate_if(is.character, as.factor)

evo.index <- tbl4pca %>%
  separate(name, 
           c("strain", "condition", "timePoint", "replicate", "pairEnd"),
           sep = "_",
           remove = FALSE) %>%
  filter(pairEnd == "1.fq.gz") %>% 
  rowid_to_column("index") %>% 
  filter(condition == "evo") %>% 
  select(index) %>% 
  pull()


Evolved lineages


dds.evo.tc <- DESeq2::DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(countData = counts[,evo.index],
                                      colData = coldata[evo.index,],
                                      design = ~ strain    +
                                        timePoint          + 
                                        strain:timePoint)

#Set rev as control of condition
dds.evo.tc$strain = relevel(dds.evo.tc$strain, "ct_evo")

#Set 0h as control of timepoint
dds.evo.tc$timePoint = relevel(dds.evo.tc$timePoint, "0h")

dds.evo.tc <- DESeq(dds.evo.tc,
                    test = "LRT",
                    reduced = ~ strain + timePoint)

res.evo.tc <- results(dds.evo.tc)


Reverted lineages


coldata <- tbl4design %>%
  separate(name, 
           c("strain0", "condition0", "timePoint", "replicate", "pairEnd"),
           sep = "_",
           remove = TRUE) %>%
  filter(pairEnd == "1.fq.gz") %>% 
  mutate(samples = paste(strain0, 
                         condition0, 
                         timePoint,
                         replicate,
                         sep = "_")) %>%
  mutate(strain = paste(strain0, condition0, sep = "_")) %>% 
  select(samples, strain, timePoint) %>%
  mutate_if(is.character, as.factor)

rev.index <- tbl4pca %>%
  separate(name, 
           c("strain", "condition", "timePoint", "replicate", "pairEnd"),
           sep = "_",
           remove = FALSE) %>%
  filter(pairEnd == "1.fq.gz") %>% 
  rowid_to_column("index") %>% 
  filter(condition == "rev") %>% 
  select(index) %>% 
  pull()

dds.rev.tc <- DESeq2::DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(countData = counts[, rev.index],
                                             colData = coldata[rev.index, ],
                                             design = ~ strain    +
                                               timePoint          + 
                                               strain:timePoint)

#Set rev as control of condition
dds.rev.tc$strain = relevel(dds.rev.tc$strain, "ct_rev")

#Set 0h as control of timepoint
dds.rev.tc$timePoint = relevel(dds.rev.tc$timePoint, "0h")


# REV DEG over the time

dds.rev.tc <- DESeq(dds.rev.tc,
                    test = "LRT",
                    reduced = ~ strain + timePoint)

res.rev.tc <- results(dds.rev.tc)

Heatmap

Heatmap is useful to see patterns in expression.


Evolved lineages


betas.evo.tc <- coef(dds.evo.tc)
colnames(betas)

# topGenes.evo.tc <- head(order(res.evo.tc$padj),50)
topGenes.evo.tc <- res.evo.tc %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  filter(!is.na(res.evo.tc$padj) & padj < 0.05 & abs(log2FoldChange) >= 1) %>%
  rownames()

mat.evo.tc <- betas.evo.tc[topGenes.evo.tc, -1]
thr <- 3
mat.evo.tc[mat.evo.tc < -thr] <- -thr
mat.evo.tc[mat.evo.tc > thr] <- thr
# Remove NAs
mat.evo.tc %<>%
  as_tibble() %>% 
  mutate(gene_id = rownames(mat.evo.tc)) %>% 
  drop_na() %>% column_to_rownames("gene_id") %>% 
  as.matrix()

#675 genes in heatmap for REV
pheatmap(mat.evo.tc,
         scale = "none",
         breaks=seq(from=-thr, to=thr, length=101),
         cluster_col=TRUE,
         show_rownames = FALSE,
         filename = "deg.evo.tc.pdf")


Reverted lineages


betas.rev.tc <- coef(dds.rev.tc)
colnames(betas)

# topGenes.evo.tc <- head(order(res.evo.tc$padj),50)
topGenes.rev.tc <- res.rev.tc %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  filter(!is.na(res.rev.tc$padj) & padj < 0.05 & abs(log2FoldChange) >= 1) %>%
  rownames()

mat.rev.tc <- betas.rev.tc[topGenes.rev.tc, -1]
thr <- 3
mat.rev.tc[mat.rev.tc < -thr] <- -thr
mat.rev.tc[mat.rev.tc > thr] <- thr
# Remove NAs
mat.rev.tc %<>%
  as_tibble() %>% 
  mutate(gene_id = rownames(mat.rev.tc)) %>% 
  drop_na() %>% column_to_rownames("gene_id") %>% 
  as.matrix()

#312 genes in heatmap for REV
pheatmap(mat.rev.tc,
         scale = "none",
         breaks=seq(from=-thr, to=thr, length=101),
         cluster_col=TRUE,
         show_rownames = FALSE,
         filename = "deg.rev.tc.pdf")


Permanova

Permanova identify main explanatory variables


Evolved lineages


colnames(counts.no.rRNA.mrn) <- coldata$samples
coldata <- as_factor(coldata)
counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t <- t(counts.no.rRNA.mrn)

perm4.strn.tmpnt.int <- adonis(counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t[evo.index,] ~ strain + 
                                 timePoint +
                                 strain * timePoint,
       data = coldata[evo.index,],
       permutations = 999,
       method = "euclidean")


Reverted lineages


perm4.strn.tmpnt.int.rev <- adonis(counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t[rev.index,] ~ strain + 
                                     timePoint +
                                     strain * timePoint,
                               data = coldata[rev.index,],
                               permutations = 999,
                               method = "euclidean")

db-RDA

Is used to remove variables effects.


Evolved lineages


coldata_shift <- coldata %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), fct_shift)) %>%
  mutate(strain = fct_shift(strain, 5))

dbrda.strain.evo <- capscale(counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t[evo.index,] 
                                ~ Condition(strain) + timePoint,
                             data = coldata_shift[evo.index,])

dbrda.time.evo <- capscale(counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t[evo.index,] 
                               ~ Condition(timePoint) + strain, 
                          data = coldata[evo.index,])

coldata_shift <- coldata %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), fct_shift)) %>%
  mutate(strain = fct_shift(strain, 5))

dbrda.strain.time.evo <- capscale(counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t[evo.index,]
                                  ~ Condition(strain + timePoint) + strain:timePoint,
                           data = coldata_shift[evo.index,])


Reverted lineages


dbrda.strain.rev <- capscale(counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t[rev.index,] 
                               ~ Condition(strain) + timePoint, 
                             data = coldata[rev.index,])

dbrda.time.rev <- capscale(counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t[rev.index,] 
                              ~ Condition(timePoint) + strain,
                           data = coldata[rev.index,])

dbrda.strain.time.rev <- capscale(counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t[rev.index,]
                                  ~ Condition(strain + timePoint) + 
                                    strain:timePoint,
                                  data = coldata_shift[rev.index,])

plot(dbrda.strain.time.rev)

Pathway

Enrichment analysis that identify pathways affected by conditions.

  • This functional annotation was done in FungiDB. Below are summarised the steps done:
      1. Access the website: https://fungidb.org/fungidb/app/
      1. Choose searches > Genes > Annotation > Gene ID
      1. Submit IDs of interest
      1. Choose analyze results
      1. Choose Metabolic Pathway Enrichment Analysis
      1. Select
      • Organism: Aspergillus fumigatus af293
      • Pathway source: KEGG, MetaCyc
      • p-value cutoff: 0.05
      • Exact match only: Yes
    • Submit
    • Download results
    • Generate figures

3 RESULTS

From quality to counts

The results are available here: MultiQC_results

  • In summary:
    • More than 20*10^6 reads per sample
    • We have high quality reads (Phred score > 30), which present high amount of duplications.
    • No adapters identified.
    • More than 95% of reads survived after trimming.
    • More than 90% of reads mapped uniquely the reference genome.
    • More than 90% of reads were assigned to exons.

PCA


  • PCA results suggests:
    • We have three groups
      • Evolved and reverted controls clustered together, in addition are clustered far from other two.
      • Lineage D evolved clustered separatelly from other two groups.
      • Evolved and Reverted lineages A and B and D reverted, together are another group.
    • D reverted 4h clustered separated and this behavior is seen in the heatmap.


Heatmap

  • The deferentially expressed genes described here explores gene expression over time. We used a full formula that models the lineage difference at time 0 (strain), the difference over time (timePoint), and any lineage-specific differences over time (strain:lineage). Besides, the reduced formula is used to remove the effect of the variables. So, we removed genes that were affected by isolated strains and isolated time points, allowing to see genes that were globally affected over the time in response to the treatment.

  • Evolved and reverted lineages seem to have a complete different response to azole based on heatmaps.

  • It seems to have a genomic difference between AB and D lineages, since d responds differently from AB in at least one point of heatmap in both evolved and reverted studies.

  • Comparing Evolved and Reverted after 4h of drug treatment, evolved ones exhibit more changes than reverted. Maybe transient genomic changes that were lost after not being under artifical selection pressure??

Evolved lineages

  • It were used 675 genes

  • 1h of exposition to voriconazole seems not induce great response, with low genes being up- and down-regulated. This behavior changes when we take a look at 4h of exposition: the greatest portion of genes being upregulated.

  • Lineage d vs ct doesn’t fit with other vs. ct lineages, seeming to have its basal expression profile like it was being exposed to voriconazole between 1-4h. Lineage A and B when vs. ct have higher number of genes upregulated.

  • At 1h of drug exposition, lineages A, B and D are already reacting to the azole and all of them seem to share the same transcriptional profiling.

  • 4h after being exposed to voriconazole, lineages A, B and D had readapted their metabolism, with most genes downregulated and less genes being upregulated.

Reverted lineages

  • We used 312 genes

  • After 1h of drug treatment, lineages A, B and D have a low number of genes being modulated, furthermore, they seem to behave in the same way.

  • After 4h of azole exposition, A, B and D dowregulate many genes, however there is a huge difference in genes being upregulated between AB and D.

  • 1h of azole treatment is not enough to induce great changes in the transcriptional profile. More interesting is the profile at 4h, with not so many genes modulated.

  • Lineages A, B and D when compared with ct seem to behave similarly, upregulating some genes.

PERMANOVA

PERMANOVA is used to identify the main explaining variables. It tests if the centroids, similar to means, of each group are significantly different from each other. Likewise, an R2 statistic is calculated, showing the percentage of the variance explained by the groups.

Evolved lineages

  • Lineage and time point are significant variables that describe the variance of our data. Lineage is responsible for 26% of the variance and time point 20%, which are high values when coming from RNA-seq.
#
#                   Df  SumsOfSqs  MeanSqs   F.Model  R2     Pr(>F)    
# strain            3 2.0184e+12 6.7278e+11  5.5021 0.26618  0.001 ***
# timePoint         2 1.5505e+12 7.7526e+11  6.3402 0.20448  0.001 ***
# strain:timePoint  6 1.0790e+12 1.7984e+11  1.4707 0.14230  0.051 .  
# Residuals        24 2.9347e+12 1.2228e+11         0.38703           
# Total            35 7.5825e+12                    1.00000           
# ---
#   Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Reverted lineages

  • Lineage here explains 14% of the variance and time point 21%, both are significant. In addition, it seems that reverted lineages are more similar because here strains explain less the variance (similar variates less).

# 
#                   Df  SumsOfSqs  MeanSqs   F.Model  R2     Pr(>F)    
# strain            3 1.0514e+12 3.5046e+11  2.2018 0.13917  0.004 ** 
# timePoint         2 1.5989e+12 7.9943e+11  5.0225 0.21163  0.001 ***
# strain:timePoint  6 1.0846e+12 1.8076e+11  1.1356 0.14356  0.252    
# Residuals        24 3.8201e+12 1.5917e+11         0.50564           
# Total            35 7.5549e+12                    1.00000           
# ---
#   Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

db-RDA

Constrained ordinations displays the variation in the data of the explanatory variables (versus unconstrained which display all the variation in the data). An unconstrained ordination is useful for viewing overall patterns in the data, but constrained ordinations allow you to test hypotheses and discover trends that were hidden in the unconstrained ordination. It is constrained because you are directly testing the influence of explanatory variables.

db-RDA is intended to detect linear relationships, on (dis)similarities generated by measures which may be non-linear.


Evolved lineages


Strain conditioned

Here we are removing the variance that is explained by strain(Conditioned) and seeing only the remaining variance that is explained by time point (Constrained). What can’t be explained is show as unconstrained in the table below. Strain that is the conditioned variable explains 26% of the variance, what goes in agreement with Permanova results. Time point explains 20% of the variance (also goes in agreement with permanova).

  • In the figure we have blue Xs that represent the three time points, the dots represent the samples and the red crosses mean the genes. It seems that we have three distinct time points with the majority of genes in the center, suggesting that some time points explain/influence more some genes.
dbrda.strain.evo <- capscale(counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t[evo.index,] 
                                ~ Condition(strain) + timePoint,
                             data = coldata_shift[evo.index,])
                  
                Inertia Proportion Rank
Total         2.166e+11  1.000e+00     
Conditional   5.767e+10  2.662e-01    3
Constrained   4.430e+10  2.045e-01    2
Unconstrained 1.147e+11  5.293e-01   30
Inertia is mean squared Euclidean distance 
Species scores projected from ‘[’ ‘counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t’ ‘evo.index’ ‘’
Time conditioned

Since we have conditioned (removed) time, we are exploring the effect of strains (constrained). Again, the results here agree with permanova, with 26% of the variance being explained by strain.

  • In the plot, we see four crosses representing the lineages. Two of them are closer to one another, probably A and B, what are distant of the other two.
dbrda.time.evo <- capscale(counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t[evo.index,] 
                               ~ Condition(timePoint) + strain, 
                          data = coldata[evo.index,])
                          
                Inertia Proportion Rank
Total         2.166e+11  1.000e+00     
Conditional   4.430e+10  2.045e-01    2
Constrained   5.767e+10  2.662e-01    3
Unconstrained 1.147e+11  5.293e-01   30
Inertia is mean squared Euclidean distance 
Species scores projected from ‘[’ ‘counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t’ ‘evo.index’ ‘’
Strain and Time conditioned
  • The interaction factor strain:time are responsible for 14% of the variance.
                Inertia Proportion Rank
Total         2.166e+11  1.000e+00     
Conditional   1.020e+11  4.707e-01    5
Constrained   3.083e+10  1.423e-01    6
Unconstrained 8.385e+10  3.870e-01   24
Inertia is mean squared Euclidean distance 
Species scores projected from ‘[’ ‘counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t’ ‘evo.index’ ‘’

Reverted lineages


Strain conditioned
dbrda.strain.rev <- capscale(counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t[rev.index,] 
                               ~ Condition(strain) + timePoint, 
                             data = coldata[rev.index,])
                             
                Inertia Proportion Rank
Total         2.159e+11  1.000e+00     
Conditional   3.004e+10  1.392e-01    3
Constrained   4.568e+10  2.116e-01    2
Unconstrained 1.401e+11  6.492e-01   30
Inertia is mean squared Euclidean distance 
Species scores projected from ‘[’ ‘counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t’ ‘rev.index’ ‘’
Time conditioned
  • Curiously the lineages are closer when compared with the evolved ones, what supports the idea that reverted lineages are more similar.
dbrda.time.rev <- capscale(counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t[rev.index,] 
                              ~ Condition(timePoint) + strain,
                           data = coldata[rev.index,])
                           
                Inertia Proportion Rank
Total         2.159e+11  1.000e+00     
Conditional   4.568e+10  2.116e-01    2
Constrained   3.004e+10  1.392e-01    3
Unconstrained 1.401e+11  6.492e-01   30
Inertia is mean squared Euclidean distance 
Species scores projected from ‘[’ ‘counts.no.rRNA.mrn.t’ ‘rev.index’ ‘’
Strain and Time conditioned
                Inertia Proportion Rank
Total         2.159e+11  1.000e+00     
Conditional   7.572e+10  3.508e-01    5
Constrained   3.099e+10  1.436e-01    6
Unconstrained 1.091e+11  5.056e-01   24

Pathway

Here it has been shown the top 40 pathways that were modulated after 4h of voriconazole exposition compared with 0h. We have ergosterol pathway activated in addition of many other pathway envolved with sterol biosynthesis.


Evolved lineages


Upregulated genes 4h vs. 0h
Downregulated genes 4h vs. 0h


Reverted lineages


Upregulated genes 4h vs. 0h
Downregulated genes 4h vs. 0h

Common genes between Evolved and Reverted lineages

Here are summarized the pathways that were common between evolved and reverted strains.

Upregulated
Downregulated

cyp51A

It seems that cyp51A expression is the same independently of time point and strain. Maybe would be a good ideia to check the expression of other genes involved with ergosterol pathway.
